title: Alumnus

infobox.value: [/əˈlʌmnəs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")  [*ə-LUM-nəs*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") | [/əˈlʌmnə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")  [*-⁠nə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") | [/əˈlʌmnaɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")  [*-⁠nye*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") | [/əˈlʌmniː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*-⁠nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),  also [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/-naɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*-⁠nye*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")

(sg.: **alumnus** (masc) or **alumna** (fem)) are former students or [graduates](/wiki/Graduation "Graduation") of a school, college, or university. The feminine plural **alumnae** is sometimes used for groups of women, and **alums** (sg.: **alum**) or **alumns** (sg.: **alumn**) as gender-neutral alternatives. The word comes from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), meaning nurslings, pupils or foster children, derived from *alere* "to nourish".[[1]](#cite_note-1)

["Alumnus"](https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/alumnus). *[Collins dictionary](/wiki/Collins_dictionary "Collins dictionary")*. Retrieved 4 December 2023. "C17: from Latin: nursling, pupil, foster son, from *alere* to nourish"
["The State Of Corporate Alumni : 2017 Survey Results"](https://enterprisealumni.com/2017-corporate-alumni-survey/). *EnterpriseAlumni*. 2017-10-02. Retrieved 2018-10-29.

["Alumni – Definition from the Free Merriam Webster Dictionary"](http://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/alumni). Merriam-webster.com. 2010-08-13. Retrieved 2011-02-15. "1: A person who has attended or has graduated from a particular school, college, or university. 2: a person who is a former member, employee, contributor, or inmate"

["Alumnus – definition of alumnus by Macmillan dictionary"](http://www.macmillandictionary.com/dictionary/british/alumnus). Macmillandictionary.com. Retrieved 2011-02-15. "Someone who was a student at a particular school, college, or university"

John Ayto (1 January 2009). [*Word Origins*](https://books.google.com/books?id=hsRISNLSSHAC&pg=PT41). [A&C Black](/wiki/A%26C_Black "A&C Black"). p. 41. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9781408101605](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9781408101605 "Special:BookSources/9781408101605").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Alumnus](/w/index.php?title=Alumnus&redirect=no "Alumnus"))

Term for former students of a school

For the film, see [*Alumni* (film)](/wiki/Alumni_(film) "Alumni (film)"). For the Argentine football team, see [Alumni Athletic Club](/wiki/Alumni_Athletic_Club "Alumni Athletic Club"). For the Argentine rugby union team, see [Asociación Alumni](/wiki/Asociaci%C3%B3n_Alumni "Asociación Alumni").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Alma_Mater_Restored_2014.jpg/500px-Alma_Mater_Restored_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alma_Mater_Restored_2014.jpg)

The [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") [noun](/wiki/Noun "Noun") *alumnus* means "foster son" or "pupil" and is derived from the [verb](/wiki/Verb "Verb") *alere* "to nourish". B *Pictured*: [Lorado Taft](/wiki/Lorado_Taft "Lorado Taft")'s [*Alma Mater*](/wiki/Alma_Mater_(Illinois_sculpture) "Alma Mater (Illinois sculpture)") in Urbana, Illinois.

**Alumni** (sg.: **alumnus** (masc) or **alumna** (fem)) are former students or [graduates](/wiki/Graduation "Graduation") of a school, college, or university. The feminine plural **alumnae** is sometimes used for groups of women, and **alums** (sg.: **alum**) or **alumns** (sg.: **alumn**) as gender-neutral alternatives. The word comes from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), meaning nurslings, pupils or foster children, derived from *alere* "to nourish".[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Alumni may or may not have completed their degree. For example, [Burt Reynolds](/wiki/Burt_Reynolds "Burt Reynolds") was an alumnus of [Florida State University](/wiki/Florida_State_University "Florida State University") but not a graduate.

Alumni can also be former inmates, former employees, former contributors, or former members of an organization.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-merriam-webster-3)[[4]](#cite_note-macmill-4)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alumni&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") [noun](/wiki/Noun "Noun") *alumnus* means "foster son" or "pupil". It is derived from the Latin [verb](/wiki/Verb "Verb") *alere* "to nourish".[[5]](#cite_note-mw-5) Separate, but from the same root, is the [adjective](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") *almus* "nourishing", found in the phrase *[alma mater](/wiki/Alma_mater "Alma mater")*, a title for a person's home university.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Pronunciation[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

|  | alumnus | alumna | alumni | alumnae |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [English](/wiki/Traditional_English_pronunciation_of_Latin "Traditional English pronunciation of Latin") | [/əˈlʌmnəs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")  [*ə-LUM-nəs*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") | [/əˈlʌmnə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")  [*-⁠nə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") | [/əˈlʌmnaɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")  [*-⁠nye*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") | [/əˈlʌmniː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*-⁠nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),  also [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/-naɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*-⁠nye*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") |
| [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") ([Classical](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin")) | [[aˈlʊmnʊs]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin") | [[aˈlʊmna]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin") | [[aˈlʊmniː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin") | [[aˈlʊmnae̯]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin") |
| [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") ([Ecclesiastical](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Latin "Ecclesiastical Latin")) | [[aˈlumnus]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin") | [[aˈlumna]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin") | [[aˈlumni]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin") | [[aˈlumne]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin") |

## Usage in Roman law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alumni&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Usage in Roman law")]

In Latin, *alumnus* is a legal term ([Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law")) to describe a child placed in [fosterage](/wiki/Fosterage "Fosterage").[[9]](#cite_note-9) According to [John Boswell](/wiki/John_Boswell "John Boswell"), the word "is nowhere defined in relation to status, privilege, or obligation."[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoswell1988116-10) Citing the research of [Henri Leclercq](/wiki/Henri_Leclercq "Henri Leclercq"), [Teresa Nani](/w/index.php?title=Teresa_Nani&action=edit&redlink=1 "Teresa Nani (page does not exist)"), and [Beryl Rawson](/wiki/Beryl_Rawson "Beryl Rawson"), who studied the many inscriptions about *alumni,* Boswell concluded that it referred to [exposed children](/wiki/Infant_exposure "Infant exposure") who were taken into a household where they were "regarded as somewhere between an heir and a slave, partaking in different ways of both categories." Despite the warmth of feelings between the parent and child, "an *alumnus* might be treated both as a beloved child and as a household servant."[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoswell1988117–119-11)

## Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alumni&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Usage")]

An alumnus or alumna is a former student or a graduate of an educational institution (school, college, university).[[12]](#cite_note-American_Heritage_Dictionary-12) According to the [United States Department of Education](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Education "United States Department of Education"), the term *alumnae* is used in conjunction with either [women's colleges](/wiki/Women%27s_colleges "Women's colleges")[[13]](#cite_note-13) or a female group of students. The term *alumni* is used in conjunction with either [men's colleges](/wiki/Men%27s_colleges "Men's colleges"), a male group of students, or a mixed group of students:

> In accordance with the rules of grammar governing the inflexion of nouns in the [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"), the masculine plural alumni is correctly used for groups composed of both sexes: *the alumni of Princeton University*.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The term is sometimes informally shortened to "alum" (optional plural "alums").[[15]](#cite_note-15) This is increasingly being used more formally as a gender-neutral alternative.[[5]](#cite_note-mw-5) However, and for this latter purpose, the option "alumn" and "alumns" are also used in some institutions in Australia, Europe and the UK.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The words "alum/alums" and "alumn/alumns" (pronounced with a silent "n") are both pronounced with the accent on the second syllable (al-UM), as opposed to the chemical compound [alum](/wiki/Alum "Alum") and its plural, "alums" (pron. AL-um).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Many universities have alumni offices that coordinate [fundraising](/wiki/Fundraising "Fundraising") and offer benefits to registered alumni. Alumni [reunions](/wiki/Class_Reunion "Class Reunion") are popular events at many institutions. These may be organized by alumni offices or by [alumni associations](/wiki/Alumni_association "Alumni association"), and are often social occasions for [fundraising](/wiki/Fundraising "Fundraising"). Full membership of alumni associations is sometimes limited just to graduates rather than all alumni, e.g. at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Universities with validation agreements may limit some alumni benefits to graduates who studied at that university rather than at validated institutions.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English"), the terms "[old boy](/wiki/Old_Boys "Old Boys")" or "old girl" are often preferred for a former pupil of a primary or secondary school, while universities refer to their former students as alumni.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Some universities, including the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), the [University of California, San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco") and [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), include former [postdoctoral researchers](/wiki/Postdoctoral_researcher "Postdoctoral researcher") as alumni, in recognition of the trainee status of such positions.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Others, such as the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology"), consider them 'associate alumni', without full access to alumni benefits.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Some higher education institutions, particularly those with a history as women's colleges or women-only universities (such as [St. Hilda’s College](/wiki/St._Hilda%27s_College,_Oxford "St. Hilda's College, Oxford"), [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford")), have chosen to retain the terms *alumna* (fem. sg.) and *alumnae* (fem. pl.) after becoming [co-ed](/wiki/Co-ed "Co-ed"), reflecting institutional continuity with their historical identity.
